title: U.S. inflation unexpectedly accelerates to 4.1% in February, dashing rate-cut hopes
topicContext: EUR/USD is highly sensitive to U.S. inflation data because CPI readings directly influence Fed rate expectations. Higher-than-expected inflation pushes back the timeline for rate cuts, widens the U.S.-Europe rate differential in the dollar's favor, and strengthens the dollar against the euro. The market reaction to CPI surprises is typically immediate and can move EUR/USD by 50-100 pips within minutes. The current consensus is that inflation is on a slow but steady path toward the Fed's 2% target — any data point that challenges this narrative forces a rapid repricing of the entire rate curve.
body:
U.S. consumer prices rose 0.5% in February from the prior month, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported Wednesday, well above the 0.3% increase economists had forecast. Year-over-year inflation accelerated to 4.1% from 3.8% in January, the first increase in the annual rate since September and a sharp setback for the Federal Reserve's efforts to bring price growth back to its 2% target.

Core inflation, which strips out volatile food and energy prices, rose 0.4% month-over-month, also above expectations. The annual core rate held steady at 3.9%, defying forecasts for a decline to 3.7%. Shelter costs, which make up roughly a third of the CPI basket, rose 0.6% for the second consecutive month, confounding expectations that housing inflation would moderate as new rental data fed through.

Markets reacted violently. The dollar index surged 1.1% in the 30 minutes after the release, its largest intraday move since March 2023. EUR/USD dropped from 1.0890 to 1.0780. Two-year Treasury yields jumped 14 basis points to 5.02%, while the 10-year yield rose 10 basis points to 4.55%.

Fed funds futures immediately repriced the outlook for rate cuts. Before the report, markets had priced in approximately two quarter-point cuts by year-end. After the report, the implied probability of even one cut by December fell to 35%. Some traders began pricing in the possibility of an additional rate hike.

The S&P 500 fell 1.8%, with rate-sensitive sectors leading the decline. Real estate investment trusts dropped 3.2%, and the Russell 2000 small-cap index fell 2.5% as higher borrowing costs weigh disproportionately on smaller companies.

Economists were divided on whether the data represented a temporary setback or a more fundamental problem. 'The disinflation story is not dead, but it's in the intensive care unit,' said the chief U.S. economist at a major bank. Others were more sanguine, pointing to base effects and arguing that shelter inflation is a lagging indicator that will eventually catch up to real-time rental data showing moderating prices.

The White House said the president 'remains focused on lowering costs for American families' and declined to comment on the implications for monetary policy. The next CPI report, covering March, is due April 10.
